notes.anchor: I omitted nothing in your honour that could be set down either as a reward of valour or as a tribute in words
notes.note: Cicero is reporting the outcome of the senatorial debate narrated in Fam. 10.12: his motion to award Plancus a public thanksgiving (supplicatio) and other honors passed after he defeated the opposition of Servilius and the veto of P. Titius.

The senatus consultum formally recorded the decree; Cicero says it was written out verbatim from his prepared motion (de scripto dicta sententia), an unusually formal procedure signaling the gravity of the honors.

notes.anchor: You will join the last stage of the work to the first
notes.note: A military metaphor: having already demonstrated his republican loyalty by crossing the Rhone, Plancus must complete the task by crushing Antony — the last act closing the circle of service.

notes.anchor: it was not Ajax, not Achilles, but Ulysses whom Homer called
notes.note: Cicero quotes the Homeric epithet ptoliporthion ("sacker of cities"), applied to Odysseus/Ulysses; Ajax and Achilles were supreme fighters but Odysseus — the strategist who devised the Trojan Horse — actually ended the war. The implication is that Plancus, like Ulysses, must be the man who finishes the job.
